paper_title: Benthic and Algal Bloom Study Methods

methods.description: Method for studying the benthos and benthic fish community using underwater photoquadrats and analyzing substrate composition, specifically diatom mat presence.

steps.description: At each island/peninsular area, we set up a 10 m 2 area to study the benthos and the benthic fish community.Sites were chosen based on appropriate benthic fish habitat, were consistent (3-5 m deep, rocky reef, dominated by boulders of all sizes) across the GOC, and were representative of the reefs in each area.
steps.searchable_summary: Select and set up study sites for benthic surveys based on habitat criteria
inputs: Site selection criteria (depth, habitat type, consistency, representativeness)

steps.description: Using a pvc camera frame and SCUBA gear, we photographed the benthos at nine nonrandom locations within the study area (three regularly spaced rows of three regularly spaced photos).Photoquadrats from the 2009 expedition are 0.25 m 2 , and those from 2010 are 0.35 m 2 .
steps.searchable_summary: Capture underwater photoquadrats of benthos using SCUBA and camera frame

steps.description: In the lab, all images were analyzed in PhotoGrid 1.0, where the substrate was described at fifty stratified random points per image.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze photoquadrat images using point count method in PhotoGrid software
outputs: Point-based substrate descriptions for each image

steps.description: For the purposes of this study, each point was given a value of 1 (= diatom mat) or 0 (= no diatom mat), and percent cover was calculated for all images.
steps.searchable_summary: Classify points as diatom mat presence/absence and calculate percent cover per image
outputs: Diatom mat presence/absence classification per point
Percent cover of diatom mat per image

methods.method_name: Algal Bloom Sample Processing for Microscopy
methods.description: Procedure for collecting, preserving, and preparing algal bloom samples for microscopic analysis, specifically Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM).

steps.description: After the opportunistic discovery of the algal bloom at Isla San Esteban in both 2009 and 2010, we collected samples that were preserved in the field in 10% formalin or 90% ethanol and others that were returned to the lab without preservation (in seawater).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect algal bloom samples using different preservation methods (formalin, ethanol, unpreserved)

steps.description: In the lab, filamentous material and supernatant (viscous liquid secreted by the filamentous material) were extracted from the non-preserved (seawater) sample and prepared for further analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract filamentous material and supernatant from unpreserved algal bloom samples in the lab

steps.description: The material was washed once with tap water to remove excess salt, plated on a microscope slide, and viewed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM).
steps.searchable_summary: Wash extracted material, prepare microscope slide, and view using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)

steps.description: Preserved samples are stored at Scripps Institution of Oceanography, La Jolla, CA, USA.
